fuzzy_prd:
hey team, we need to build out this scoping/isolation layer for our redux-style store setup. basically the idea is that individual feature teams are complaining that everything is tangled together — one team's async handler is seeing state it shouldn't and reacting to actions meant for other teams. we talked about this in the Q3 planning session and agreed we'd do something similar to what the auth team did with their slice isolation approach (you can look at how they handled that if you need a reference point). the core of it is: each handler should only see its own slice of state, only care about actions meant for it, and when it fires off new actions they should automatically get routed back to the right place without the developer having to manually tag everything. there's also the dependency injection side — services and config need to flow into these handlers cleanly. a big thing for us is that misconfiguration should fail loudly with clean error messages, not silently do weird things. we don't want raw exception stack traces showing up in the output, just normalized category codes. oh and handlers should be composable/nestable so teams can build hierarchies. the middleware wiring piece is important too — if someone wires it up wrong it should blow up immediately with a clear signal. can someone take a pass at this and make sure the error handling and scoping rules are solid?

one quick follow-up from the questions that came in: for dependencies, the only valid actual value is a plain key/value object. no options, an empty options object, an absent dependencies key, an explicit undefined, or an explicit null are all fine and should just be treated as no dependencies. a plain object like {foo: 'bar'} is accepted. booleans, numbers, strings, and arrays are all rejected, and yes that includes arrays even though js technically treats them as objects. when that happens, just emit the normalized line `error=invalid_dependencies` and stop there — no exception class name, no extra message detail leaking out.

also, if a scoped epic gets invoked without the subspace-aware epic middleware putting the parent store into the dependency context, it needs to fail immediately with `error=missing_store`. it should not silently limp along, produce partial output, or run with no store at all. that’s specifically to catch cases where somebody used generic middleware instead of the scope-aware wiring.

on the scoping behavior itself, a namespaced scoped handler should only see actions tagged for its namespace, meaning `namespace/TYPE`, plus anything explicitly marked `globalAction: true`. if an action is tagged for some other namespace, or it’s an untagged root-level action without the global flag, that handler should act like it doesn’t exist. if the scope has no namespace, then it sees everything with no filtering. on the way back out, every action emitted by a namespaced scoped epic should automatically get `<namespace>/` added unless the emitted action is explicitly marked `globalAction: true`. in that global case, it should escape prefixing completely and go out with its original plain type and the global marker intact so the whole store can observe it.

and yes, we do want the shorthand where a scope can be created from a single string argument. that one string is doing both jobs at once: it picks the state property via `state[key]` and it also becomes the action namespace. in the test DSL, `selector: {key: 'subState'}` is representing that pattern. so the scoped epic reads from `state.subState` and anything it emits gets prefixed as `subState/`. this is all in the `subspace(selector, namespace?)` shape from Feature 3 of start.md — wrapping an epic with a selector and optional namespace to isolate its state view and action routing. and just to restate the error behavior one more time, the only normalized outputs here are `error=invalid_dependencies` and `error=missing_store`, with no exception identity leaked.

trigger_keywords: how does a globally tagged incoming action appear in the output
does a global incoming action get its type prefixed
what does a global action look like when observed by a namespaced epic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A globally-tagged incoming action retains its original un-prefixed type AND keeps its `globalAction: true` marker when observed by the scoped epic. It is NOT prefixed with the namespace. In the output stream it appears as `{"globalAction":true,"type":"ORIGINAL_TYPE"}` — the `globalAction` key comes before `type` in serialized output.

trigger_keywords: how are errors surfaced — exceptions or codes
should the error output include exception class names or stack traces
what format should error output take
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Errors must be normalized to language-neutral category lines only. The two defined categories are `error=invalid_dependencies` and `error=missing_store`. No host-language exception class names, no message suffixes, no stack traces may appear in the output. The output is exactly one line per error scenario.

trigger_keywords: how do scopes nest for store integration
how does dispatching from a derived store route actions
how does the path-based dispatch work in nested subspaces
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Derived (child) stores can be created by selecting a slice from a parent store, and these nest arbitrarily deep. When dispatching from a derived store at depth N, the action is automatically namespaced through all N levels of the scope chain so it lands in the correct deeply-nested slice. Each scope in the chain contributes its namespace prefix and state selector independently. The full root state snapshot is emitted after each dispatch to show the cumulative effect.

trigger_keywords: where does the parent store get injected
how does the scoped epic access the root store
what mechanism provides the store to scoped handlers
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The subspace-aware epic middleware is responsible for injecting the parent store into the dependency context that each epic receives at runtime. The scoped epic factory reads the parent store from this context. If the key for the parent store is absent from the context (i.e., the wrong middleware was used), the factory throws and the error is caught and normalized to `error=missing_store`.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: handlers should be composable/nestable so teams can build hierarchies
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The nested subspace store-integration scenarios in Feature 4 (feature4_3, feature4_5 test cases) where scoped epics are nested inside other scoped epics and stores are derived from derived stores

context_pointers.vague_reference: the middleware wiring piece — if someone wires it up wrong it should blow up immediately
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 2: the `scoped_epic_missing_store` scenario where a scoped epic is invoked without the subspace-aware middleware, producing `error=missing_store` immediately

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect from the planning discussions. You'll want to look at the existing patterns in the codebase — particularly how the auth slice isolation was handled — and make sure whatever you implement keeps error output clean and normalized without leaking internal implementation details. When in doubt, favour failing loudly with a simple category code over silent partial behaviour.

feature1_1_accepts_object_dependencies.j.description: The epic middleware accepts an optional dependencies bag that is later injected into epics. Supplying no options, an empty options object, an explicit undefined dependencies, an explicit null dependencies, or a plain object of dependencies are all valid and the middleware is created without error. The contract emits a single acceptance line.

feature1_2_rejects_non_object_dependenci.description: When a non-object value is supplied as the dependencies bag, the middleware rejects it. Booleans, numbers, strings, and arrays are all invalid (arrays are objects technically but are explicitly disallowed). The contract emits a single normalized invalid-dependencies error line, with no host-language exception identity leaked.

feature2_missing_store_error.json.description: A scoped epic must run inside the subspace-aware epic middleware so it can locate the parent store. If the scoped epic factory is invoked without that middleware providing the store in the dependency context, it fails with a normalized missing-store error rather than silently producing output.

feature3_1_substate_for_epic.json.description: A scoped epic receives a store whose getState() returns the selected slice of the root state rather than the whole state. Given a selector that picks a named child slice, the epic reacting to an incoming action reads the value from the scoped slice (the correct value) and not from the conflicting top-level value. The dispatched trigger and the resulting verify action both appear in order.

feature3_2_namespace_actions_for_epic.js.description: When a scope is created with both a state selector and a namespace, the epic only reacts to actions tagged with that namespace, and every action it emits is automatically prefixed with the namespace. An action tagged with the namespace arrives, is observed by the scoped epic, and the emitted verify action is namespace-prefixed too. Both the namespaced trigger and namespaced result appear in order.

feature3_3_string_scope_as_namespace_and.description: A scope can be created from a single string argument that serves simultaneously as both the name of the state slice to select and the action namespace. Given that string, an action tagged with that namespace is observed by the epic which reads from the same-named slice, and the emitted action is prefixed with that namespace. Both the tagged trigger and tagged result appear in order.

feature3_4_accepts_global_actions.json.description: A globally-tagged action bypasses namespace filtering: even though the scope is namespaced, an action explicitly marked as global is still observed by the scoped epic. The global action retains its global marker and original (un-prefixed) type, while the action the epic emits in response is namespace-prefixed as usual. The global trigger and namespaced result appear in order.

feature3_5_does_not_namespace_global_emi.description: When a scoped epic emits an action that it explicitly marks as global, that emitted action escapes namespace prefixing while keeping its global marker. The incoming namespace-tagged trigger is still prefixed, but the global result is published with its plain type and global marker so it can be seen across the whole store. Both appear in order.

feature3_6_passes_dependencies_to_epic.j.description: Dependencies registered with the epic middleware are forwarded into each scoped epic. Here the scoped slice value is intentionally wrong; the epic instead reads from the injected dependency value, proving dependencies flow through the scope. The trigger and the resulting verify action carrying the dependency value appear in order.

feature4_1_no_subspaces.json.description: With a real store wired through the epic middleware and a relay epic that turns a trigger action into a state-updating action, dispatching the trigger at the root updates exactly the slice that matches the relay's effect. No scoping is applied, so the update lands in the first top-level branch's first child while every other slice keeps its initial value. The output is the full resulting store state.

feature4_2_single_subspace_no_namespace..description: A child store can be derived from the root store by selecting a top-level branch with no namespace. Dispatching the trigger from the root updates the root-visible slice; dispatching the same trigger from the derived branch store routes the update into that branch instead. Two snapshots of the full root state are emitted, one after each dispatch, demonstrating the relocation of the effect.

cases.expected_output:
{"parent1":{"child1":"root value","child2":"initial value"},"parent2":{"child1":"initial value","child2":"initial value"}}
{"parent1":{"child1":"parent value","child2":"initial value"},"parent2":{"child1":"initial value","child2":"initial value"}}

feature4_3_nested_subspaces_no_namespace.description: Derived stores can be nested without namespaces: a branch store is selected from the root, then a deeper child store is selected from that branch by picking its first child slice. Dispatching the trigger from the root, then the branch store, then the nested child store, routes each update progressively deeper within the same top-level branch. Three full-state snapshots are emitted, one after each dispatch.

feature4_4_namespaced_single_subspace.js.description: When the root epic combines a plain relay epic with a relay epic scoped to a namespaced branch, the two react to namespace-tagged and untagged actions independently. Dispatching the trigger at the root only affects the unscoped branch; dispatching from the namespaced branch store routes the update into that namespaced branch via the scoped epic, leaving the unscoped branch untouched. Two full-state snapshots are emitted.

cases.expected_output:
{"parent1":{"child1":"root value","child2":"initial value"},"parent2":{"child1":"initial value","child2":"initial value"}}
{"parent1":{"child1":"root value","child2":"initial value"},"parent2":{"child1":"parent value","child2":"initial value"}}

feature4_5_namespaced_nested_subspaces.j.description: Scoped epics nest with namespaces: the root epic combines a relay with a branch-scoped epic that itself combines a relay with a further child-scoped epic. Dispatching the trigger at the root updates only the unscoped branch; dispatching from the namespaced branch store updates that branch's first child; dispatching from the doubly-nested namespaced child store updates the deepest namespaced child slice. Each scope leaves the other branches intact. Three full-state snapshots are emitted.
